posts.name: David Haycock
posts.cooked:
Good day Berserkers!

I don’t know much about Beaujolais except that my wife enjoys them and has been asking about finding a tasting that will have some. And since I buy the majority of the wine, keeping wifey happy allows me to continue buying new bottles that the enablers on this board turn me on to! I’d be very much appreciative of some recommendations for bottles primarily below $30 but would consider some really good juice up to about $50.

I thank thee in advance!

Hi David,

Lapierre, Foillard, Thivin, Metras and a few others are considered at the top. I’d look for the Lapierre Morgon and Thivin Brouilly, pretty sure both can be found for $30 or less.

Other producers I like are Vissoux, JP Brun, Jadot, and Drouhin. Drouhin Morgon is a steal for the quality, imho. Really though, there’s an ocean of good Beaujolais and some of the recent vintages have been good to outstanding. Best part is it’s not an expensive pursuit and you can really sample a broad range without dropping serious $'s.
The 2012 Julien Sunier Regnie and Fleurie are wonderful right now. Daniel Bouland & the JP Brun wines are fairly easy to find and are great values under $30.

posts.name: Matt Latuchie
posts.cooked:
Coudert, Coudert, Coudert.

One that doesn’t get talked about much is Domaine des Billards from Saint Amour.

posts.name: Brad B a l l i n g e r
posts.cooked: +1 on Thivn and Foillard. Also, Cedric Chignard, Nicole Chanrion, Georges Descombes, Chantal et Eric Appert-Coudert, Paul Cinquin. These are all going to be Cru Beaujolais.

posts.name: Mark B
posts.cooked:
David J. Haycock:

Good day Berserkers!

I don’t know much about Beaujolais except that my wife enjoys them and has been asking about finding a tasting that will have some. And since I buy the majority of the wine, keeping wifey happy allows me to continue buying new bottles that the enablers on this board turn me on to! I’d be very much appreciative of some recommendations for bottles primarily below $30 but would consider some really good juice up to about $50.

I thank thee in advance!

Hi David,

If you’re new to Beaujolais, I recommend starting with current vintages of Georges Duboeuf’s different Cru bottlings (Fleurie, Chénas, Morgon, Brouilly, Régnié, etc). Don’t expect to have your socks knocked off, but do expect to have a great deal of fun exploring the 10 Crus at sub $20 per bottle. Only from there would I then proceed to explore more expensive domaine bottlings.

Matt Latuchie:

Coudert, Coudert, Coudert.

One that doesn’t get talked about much is Domaine des Billards from Saint Amour.

Good to hear on Coudert, bought some 2013s.

posts.name: T. Riley
posts.cooked:
Most of the best producers have already mentioned and I heartily endorse them.

Someone to consider who doesn’t get a lot of press, but makes wines that are generally not hard to find and, with all respect to Mark, I think are a big step up from Georges Duboeuf is Stephane Aviron. A negociant operation, but a particularly careful one, in my experience.

(Full disclosure: I sell Mr. Aviron’s wines. But honestly, I’d be a fan even if I didn’t)

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: I have a couple of cases of each of the 2012 Georges Descombes Régnié and 2010 Georges Descombes Fleurie Vieilles Vignes that serve me very well as a house red at the moment.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Not to drift excessively, by the way, but how are the 2013s?

David - Vissoux is another good producer: the Traditionelle comes in under $20 and is made from very old vines (check out the website).

Many cru bottlings from better producers need a couple or few years to come 'round - Coudert’s basic Fleurie, for example.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked: Brun, descombes, Coudert, Metras if you can find it, foillard, lapierre are my go tos.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: I agree with the rec on Aviron (and I don’t sell it). Check to see if chambers street wines has any of the '07 or '05 Pierre Savoye Morgon cote de Puy left. Hard to beat for $17.99. The pignard 2012 morgon that they import is also nice for the same price.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
A.Gillette:

I agree with the rec on Aviron (and I don’t sell it). Check to see if chambers street wines has any of the '07 or '05 Pierre Savoye Morgon cote de Puy left. Hard to beat for $17.99. The pignard 2012 morgon that they import is also nice for the same price.

I found the '07 Savoye a bit disappointing. Not bad, but I expected more from a producer I like in one of my favorite vintages. I’ve also never been that impressed by Aviron compared with many of the other producers suggested in this thread.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: Fair enough. I thought the Savoye was awesome. Went back and bought more. Judging by how quickly the 4 cases disappeared, someone else must have liked them as well.

posts.name: David Haycock
posts.cooked:
Thank you everyone for the recommendations. This provides a dearth of options to begin exploring. We will be near a Total Wine today (I have my local wine shop also), would they have any of these mentioned?

If not, my local store can order most things in.

Thanks again.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
David, I just pulled up the Palm Springs TW site, and it doesn’t look like they carry the producers mentioned in this thread, except Jadot - but only the villages is mentioned. They do have Quatre Vents Fleurie, which I think I’ve heard okay things about. In FL, though, you have some better stores (e.g., B-21) that may be within driving distance or offer good in-state shipping deals.

I’d repeat that many cru Beaujolais from good makes deserve a few years in the bottle. Vissoux’s Traditionelle and Brun’s l’Ancien are above-average village batters that you can enjoy pretty young.

posts.name: todd waldmann
posts.cooked: According to John Gilman, 2013 is a very solid vintage with classically styled wines. The few I have tasted are consistent with that assertion.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: There is way too much excellent Beaujolais out there to bother with anything from the industrial giant, Duboeuf.

David M. Bueker:

There is way too much excellent Beaujolais out there to bother with anything from the industrial giant, Duboeuf.

+lots, especially if you’re into Beaujolais enough to ask or check out a wine board for recs.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Here’s my list of recommendable Beaujolais producers:

  1. Very structured and for long time ageing
  • Château du Moulin-à-Vent
  • Château des Jacques (Louis Jadot) - these need ages to come around in my opinion
  • Thibault Liger-Belair
  • Domaine Labruyère
  • Clos de MEZ
  • Xavier & Nicolas Barbet / Château Bellevue
  1. The classics
  • Marcel Lapierre
  • Jean Foillard
  • Coudert / Clos de la Roilette
  • Georges Descombes
  • Château Thivin
  • Domaine de la Voute des Crozes (Nicole Chanrion)
  • Daniel Bouland
  • Louis-Claude Desvignes
  • Jean-Marc Burgaud
  • Bernard Diochon
  • Paul Janin
  • Dominique Piron
  • Domaine Chignard
  1. The wines everyone can agree to and that are widely available
  • Domaine de Vissoux (Chermette)
  • Jean-Paul Brun
  1. The rich ones
  • Jean-Claude Lapalu
  • Lucien Lardy
  • Domaine de la Madone (Jean-Marc Desprès)
  1. The delicate ones
  • Yvon Mêtras
  • Domaine de la Grand Cour (Jean-Louis Dutraive)
  1. The up and coming
  • Jules Desjourneys
  • Julien Sunier
  • Paul-Henri Thillardon
  • Aurelien Grillet
  • Karim Vionnet
  • Damien Coquelet
  • Richard Rottiers
  • Raphael Chopin
  • Julie Balagny
  1. The reliable that don’t get much attention
  • Domaine des Billards (Loron)
  • Passot Collonge / Passot les Rampaux
  • Domaine de Point du Jour
  • Domaine des Nugues
  • Hubert Lapierre
  • Bernard Mêtrat
  • Olivier Merlin

Hi David,

If you’re new to Beaujolais, I recommend starting with current vintages of Georges Duboeuf’s different Cru bottlings (Fleurie, Chénas, Morgon, Brouilly, Régnié, etc). Don’t expect to have your socks knocked off, but do expect to have a great deal of fun exploring the 10 Crus at sub $20 per bottle. Only from there would I then proceed to explore more expensive domaine bottlings.

I would never do this. I really enjoy Beaujolais and find Deboeuf’s lineup to be mediocre at best. I don’t buy them even when they are available very cheap. Not good winemaking IMO, even if it is supposed to be improved. I also just don’t see the logic. Sure, it’s cheap and he makes several wines, but when superior wines are available for similar prices I don’t see it.

I like:
Foillard
Lapierre
Coudert
Thivin
Vissoux
Brun
Burgaud
Aviron (former Potel-Aviron)
Jadot’s Chateau de Jacques lineup

Edited to +1 the Coquelet recommendation.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: One that hasn’t been mentioned is Pavillon de Chavannes’ Côte de Brouilly Cuvée des Ambassades, which I think Garagiste sells every year. I’ve cut out Garagiste so I haven’t bought the last couple vintages, but I should remedy that, because it’s a really great expression of Beaujolais, with about as strong a mineral character as you can find in the region (or any red wine really).

posts.name: T. Riley
posts.cooked:
Mark,

I’m not sure anyone is writing them off, just positing that there are other wines at a pretty similar price point that deliver a lot more substance. What I will give you is that Duboeuf is unquestionably the easiest Beaujolais to find. Sadly, while the wines can often be good, I don’t think they are the sort that would get anyone excited about the region. Put differently, if I had never tasted Beaujolais and you gave me a Duboeuf (Jean Descombes or otherwise), I doubt I would see a lot of reason to explore more.

StevenB:

Here’s my list of recommendable Beaujolais producers:

Steven, awesome list, many names I haven’t heard of. Really appreciate you taking the time to share, thx.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
T. Riley:

if I had never tasted Beaujolais and you gave me a Duboeuf (Jean Descombes or otherwise), I doubt I would see a lot of reason to explore more.

Apparently millions of people feel differently about that. It’s fashionable to trash DuBoeuf but they really aren’t bad wines. I became interested in trying other producers after drinking… 1985 Morgon Jean Descombes.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
Craig Gleason:
T. Riley:

if I had never tasted Beaujolais and you gave me a Duboeuf (Jean Descombes or otherwise), I doubt I would see a lot of reason to explore more.

Apparently millions of people feel differently about that. It’s fashionable to trash DuBoeuf but they really aren’t bad wines. I became interested in trying other producers after drinking… 1985 Morgon Jean Descombes.

This is a silly debate. All of these wines being discussed are very reasonably priced. Nearly all are under $35. Most are under $25, many under $20. There is of course no harm in picking up some Duboef, but the point is that anyone dabbling in the region should absolutely also try Foillard/Lapierre/Vissoux/Brun/etc., and no one should avoid those wines because Duboef is out there. Beaujolais has the distinct advantage vis-a-vis other regions that you can try the absolute best wines without breaking the bank – there is zero reason to settle for mediocre Beaujolais.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
I would agree that trying Duboeuf’s Beaujolais is neither a great Beaujolais starting point nor educating in any positive way. At least here in Germany, the Duboeuf bottlings aren’t particularly cheap, you save maybe 2 or 3 Euros per 0.75 l bottle as compared to any good producers. I have never tried the whole range, but I also find the Duboeuf portfolio to be slightly confusing. There are Georges Duboeuf bottlings and then there are lots of different bottlings that have “Domaine” names. We had some Duboeuf made wines in vintage tastings and they almost always fell through.

In getting to know the crus, I’d rather look to Dominique Piron who has Morgon, Morgon Côte du Py, Regnié, Chénas (Chénas Quartz is one of the best Chénas out there), Moulin-à-Vent, Brouilly, Saint Amour and Fleurie, so all Crus except Chiroubles, Juliénas and Côte de Brouilly. Otherwise, Jean-Paul Brun is a good starting point who has Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie (Village + Grille Midi), Côte de Brouilly and Morgon. A third good starting point would be the wines of the Barbet family (Maison Loron) who have négociant wines as well as own wines and a great selection with Morgon (Ch. de Bellevue), Moulin-à-Vent (Nicolas & Xavier Barbet), Saint Amour (Domaine des Billards), Juliénas (Domaine de la Vieille Eglise), Fleurie (Château de Fleurie) and Côte de Brouilly and Regnié (Château de la Pierre).

posts.cooked: Selection Massale brings in Bruno Debize, haven’t had a bad bottle yet and some are just awesome.

PXL_20230814_013311694~21920×2095 300 KB

What a great cheat sheet! I’d totally slot the 2019 Jean Claude Lapalu ‘Vielles Vignes’ [Beaujolais Villages] into the same ‘rich’ category StevenB uses. 13% abv, but feels fuller/bigger than I would expect. On the low side for acidity, but it paired well enough with a slab of baked salmon. Lots of red fruit, and a bit of sediment at the end. I should have saved this to enjoy with a family member who is sulfur sensitive, but can tolerate Foillard and Pascal Aufranc, so perhaps this would have been ok for them too, as Lapalu is reputed to have a light touch with SO2. A great summer drink on a triple digit day, and I’d give it an A-. The early drinking regional AOC are about as far as I need to go into Bojo to get satisfaction.

posts.name: Emery Adoradio
posts.cooked: Adding Dom. Louis Boillot - all of their Crus.

posts.name: Bill Sweeney
posts.cooked: I bought some 2014 Billards based on good reviews, but it’s still an ugly duckling. Are these just slow to reach a drinking phase?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My experience has been they have a much faster aging trajectory than one would expect based on tasting the young wines, so yours may be more on the downslope than the upslope.

posts.cooked: I would recommend Lafarge-Vial with a, not surprisingly, very burgundian approach. The Fleurie Clos Vernyay is one of his top bottlings.

In getting to know the crus, I’d rather look to Dominique Piron who has Morgon, Morgon Côte du Py, Regnié, Chénas (Chénas Quartz is one of the best Chénas out there), Moulin-à-Vent, Brouilly, Saint Amour and Fleurie, so all Crus except Chiroubles, Juliénas and Côte de Brouilly. Otherwise, Jean-Paul Brun is a good starting point who has Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie (Village + Grille Midi), Côte de Brouilly and Morgon. A third good starting point would be the wines of the Barbet family (Maison Loron) who have négociant wines as well as own wines and a great selection with Morgon (Ch. de Bellevue), Moulin-à-Vent (Nicolas & Xavier Barbet), Saint Amour (Domaine des Billards), Juliénas (Domaine de la Vieille Eglise), Fleurie (Château de Fleurie) and Côte de Brouilly and Regnié (Château de la Pierre).

10000050061920×2644 333 KB

Over three nights, the 2019 Dom. des Billards [St Amour] is a delight! This AOC isn’t on the shelves regularly in my area - I had to mail order this - but the few vintages I’ve had before make that effort worth it. It’s a bit bigger, 14.5% abv, and more grippy than the typical Bojo I drink, but still nothing in comparison to my usual Bordeaux/Rhone consumption. Great floral bouquet, then red stone fruit on the palate, with a bit of drying tannin on the end. At age 6 it has not faded a bit over being open 3 days. I cannot find consistently Billards (or really any St Amour^) without paying for shipping but I’ll always keep my eyes open if they ever pop up. I’d give this an A- and if one prowls around other WB threads mentioning this venerable property (order books show invoices sent to Louis XVI’s finance minister!) there are comments about it’s age-ability too.

^ actually, I should try TW’s direct import.

PS: it’s dark / firm enough to be more pinot than gamay, so I had it (at least one night) with panko crusted steelhead.

10000050011920×1442 284 KB
